sentences.text: Yeah, well they were actually-- then they formed the police.
They were the police in that little town.
This, this, was right at the beginning.
It would probably be in, in, in, in Jul.
Yes.
Oh, yes.
They would knock.
I mean, you know, it would scare us all to death.
I mean, it's like they're going to break the door down.
I do remember that-- and yelled for us to come out.
And that's how--
There was-- I remember two.
In fact, one of them we knew.
You know, he was familiar.
Probably my dad knew.
There were three guys-- two guys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
They didn't come in the house, they just made us come out.
I mean, it's like we were going to get shot.
Yes, there was the wall, and then what they did-- there would be-- you know, they could get away with that.
They could do that, line us up and point guns and threaten.
I think this went on for a couple weeks.
Yeah, well, not--maybe.
I don't know whether it was that long or not.
But they kept us out, I'm sure it's about half an hour or so.
Then we-- you know, then they took off.
The first time they were not.
And no, they were not in uniform at that time.
They just-- they were a police force.
They were known as a police.
I don't know whether-- They're not even trained, but they did what they wanted to do.
Well, it seems like for a time maybe every other night.
I know there was at least three times a week we had that.
We were always scared to death.
I mean, I.
I-- you konw, it's hard to recall, but everyone was-- we were scared.
I mean, any day they thought they're going to kill us.
Next thing happened what is-- that came probably in September when they came.
There was-- you know, I heard my dad say-- he said, we have to report to the school.
He says, there's some kind of a registration.
So there was something out.
I didn't see the, the announcement.
It may have been posted.
But he said to his neighbor, he says, we have to go to register.
That's what they want us to do.
But he says, Fritz, I don't like it.
It doesn't look good.
My dad said that to this neighbor, because he too had to go.
Mostly men of a certain age-- you know, adults, men.
And I remember him telling this, this other-- this neighbor of ours that, that, I don't like it.
And I was thinking about that, and then my dad was gone.
And I think we-- they-- we came to the-- to see what happens-- what happened to these men.
They locked them all up once they gathered in the center of town.
They locked them up.
And they were locked up--
All these men-- there were 155 men.
And they, they put them in the school building in the basement.
And all these men were there.
I think it was on a Wednesday.
They were there Wednesday night, Thursday, and Friday morning.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They took them out about a kilometer approximately from the town.
There was a huge barn.
This we found out later.
And they made them-- I think the Gentiles.
They, they-- there some people dug a huge grave.
And, and on-- that was on Friday morning.
Friday around noon, we see army SS troopers arrived.
And most of the women and children were right in the center of town wondering what's happening to these men.
Just nobody [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could think that they're going to kill them.
Well, when the SS troopers came in with mounted machine guns and they dispersed in the little town and they went in from house to house-- also, if there was a man who remained, they got him out.
And they were searching for some things, and I don't remember what they were searching.
I mean, they went through every room.
They came to our house, and my brother was in my little brother's crib just kind of sitting inside that crib.
And I, I, I remember one of the SS men looked at him.
It's like he couldn't make up his mind.
He was already at that time, I think he was about 15 years old.
Zindel, yeah.
And he, he-- whether he's like the right age or not, and they looked at him, and they left him there.
They didn't do anything, and then they were gone.
Later in the afternoon, we heard machine gun fire.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And there was-- And the women were screaming and carrying on.
There was one survivor, and there was one of my cousins survived.
I, I-- his dad and my dad had him hidden in that barn, so he was the only one-- came-- late that evening, he came home.
And that was my aunt-- my aunt, it scared the hell out of her.
Because it was late at night.
He waited.
I mean, she thought that he's already dead.
She just couldn't believe that, that he survived.
Yes, she was there also.
She was there.
I mean, women were just--
Yes.
No, we were on the outside somewhere, you know.
But we heard the machine guns-- machine gun being fired.
He came, he came to his mother's house.
We found out the day after.
It was sort of a secret.
We kept it.
We, we didn't tell anybody.
Nobody knew that this happened.
We didn't tell.
And, and somehow she got him out of this little town into a city of Lutsk.
And he was working as a non-Jew for a while.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think he came to visit us one time.
And he went back.
Yeah, well, we was-- we saw the area where it was.
I mean, you know, there was just freshly dug-up dirt.
I mean, you know, we saw it.
I mean, we didn't--
Yes, yes.
All in one mass grave.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Actually, at that area were already was farmland.
And mostly Jews were not living there.
There were some Ukrainian farmers all around that area.
After that, it was just horrible.
I mean, you know, most of the men were gone, and just women and children.
And there was-- in order to, to have food we had to sell off things, whatever we had, whatever farmers wanted in trade for food.
And this went on-- that winter was a horrible winter.
And early in the spring, they told us that we have to move to another town, not a [INAUDIBLE].
And they made us leave just about everything.
All you could-- what-- whatever you could carry, you'd carried.
I don't know exactly how many miles.
Could've been 10 miles we walked.
And every one of us carried something.
So we abandoned everything-- the house, the things in there.
This was in the spring of-- yes.
And we came to this little town.
It's called Ostrozec.
And we moved in with an aunt of ours.
They, they lived in a small house.
They originally were also in a village in a-- they were farming.
And I don't know how, but they had this place in that Ostrozec, and we moved in with them.
So-- And there was some other people there.
It was just a small house.
I don't know if it was three rooms.
And they were just a lot of people in it.
So the place was crowded.
It was horrible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I remember-- I don't know how I managed to get out and work for this farmer.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to take care of the cow and some other stuff I would do.
He was-- he had a bad leg or had lost a leg.
I don't remember whether it was a wooden leg.
He couldn't do very much.
So I did most of the work for him.
And on weekends I would get some food from him-- potatoes, bread-- and bring it home.
So this went on for-- I don't know, for several-- maybe a couple months.
And at one point he said to me, there's something I need to tell you.
He said it, or course, in Ukrainian.
And he says-- I still remember how that-- he says, whether you want to hear it or not, there's something I have to tell you.
And he says, they are digging graves Ostrozec.
That means they're going to kill the Jews.
So I dumped whatever I was doing and ran towards the little town to-- so to be with my mom and sister and my little brother.
But mostly to be with my mom.
I-- and I was running all, all the way and I, I just hoped that I wouldn't be late, because I-- you know, if something's going to happen, it's going to happen with me same as mom.
And I had to run by the Jewish cemetery.
It was just the outskirts of the-- Ostrozec.
And that's where I saw them digging this huge grave-- there were some farmers doing that.
And I saw these-- and I saw that.
I, I-- it just made me so ill.
And I-- so like picturing, this is where I'm going to end up.
And I couldn't cross it.
I had to circle.
Yeah, I just couldn't face that.
I got home, and, and I was, here I am.
And my mother was very unhappy.
She says, why would you do that.
Why did you come home?
You should have stayed there.
Well, I wasn't sure whether he wanted to keep me or whatever.
And at that time, I would be scared to be any place near where they kill Jews.
Everybody was afraid to keep anybody.
But I, I wanted to be with my mom.
And I said, I'm not going anywhere.
I'm going to be with you.
Whatever happens to you is going to happen with me.
Well, couple nights-- I think the next night, we tried-- we made an attempt-- all of us, my mom-- we tried to leave the ghetto.
And I don't know where we would have gone.
Maybe in-- well, they caught us very quickly.
They stopped us.
There was a place where a lot of police-- Ukrainian police were there.
And they pushed us back into the ghetto.
The place was wired.
There was a couple gates, but we were able-- managed to lift the wire and then crawl out of there.
But they got us back in.
So, so we were waiting.
Every day it might happen.
We just-- I mean, every hour it could happen.
We don't know when and what.
And it's an awful thing to wait to die.
And-- Let's back up for a minute.
When you first found out you had to move to the ghetto, how were you notified?
I don't remember exactly.
But all I know-- I remember is we were walking from one town to-- one little town led to the other.
Well, we just-- I just remember carrying a bundle.
Yes, yeah.
But I don't remember details.
At that time, it was so difficult.
All you could see is, is just-- oh.
I mean, something horrible is going to happen.
And that's all that would stick on your mind.
I mean, even the things to take didn't mean anything, because we knew something bad is going to happen.
So it didn't matter.
So we took some-- whatever we could.
I guess [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother and my older brother carried most things.

It's-- I mean, she must have gotten something because while we were in the ghetto, she sort of arranged the next day-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gave something to a farmer who was outside the barbed wire.
I don't know how she arranged that.
And the farmer was waiting.
And she came to me and she said, come with me, and I went with her.
And we get to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fence and there I see this farmer, horse and wagon.
She says, I want you to get on, on this wagon.
And I, I had to-- I argued with her.
I said, I'm not going.
Yeah, that was-- yeah.
That was after I came home, right.
And--
Yes.
After we tried to escape, maybe a couple days after that.
That's when my mother made this-- somehow she made that arrangement.
She gave that guy something and, and forced me-- forced me to do that.
She says you must do it.
She says maybe one of us will survive.
And I got out.
And soon I got on the wagon.
It was just a horrible feeling to see my mother in pain and that look on her face crying.
We both did.
And my sister was there, my little brother, they were all-- and the guy drove off.
And I don't know, but maybe a kilometer outside, he stopped his horse.
Well, I got the picture I had to get off.
And I got off, and he took off.
So here I am walking.
I'm just totally alone.
I had no idea what to do, where to go, but I kept on walking.
I came to that first village.
I just was going through it, just a road.
And, and I remember a bunch of young kids were throwing rocks at me.
Well, I don't know, but I ran from them.
I passed that-- the little village.
And I-- and I looked for a place where there were no-- you know, there was no houses or anything, which is not to face anybody.
And I think there was some hills.
And I managed to go near the-- by these hills.
And there was a river-- by the banks of that river.
And I was crying.
I just-- I didn't know what to do.
And, and then soon night came.
And I went to-- this was in the fall, so there was harvest, and there was some straw, piles of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] hay and straw in the fields.
And I remember I just went to one of those and stayed overnight.
I slept there.
I carried nothing with me.
I had no warm clothes.
And then I kept on walking the next day.
And I was looking and tried to remember some of my dad's friends who were not Jewish.
There was a-- there was one-- this woman, that used to-- in fact, she helped raise me.
She was with us when I was a kid.
And I knew where she lived.
I remembered the, the name the village.
And I got to that and--
Lawrow was the name of it.
And she was living there.
I guess I asked people, and they asked somebody that told me where she was.
And I got to her.
And she was, was very scared.
She kept me that night.
And she told me how much she's afraid if, you know, they catch me, she may get killed too.
Why, I understood that.
So I was there overnight.
And then I left, and I looked for some other people that my dad did some business, and I remembered the names.
Did you remember this woman's name?
Pavlina was her name.
Yeah, she-- I-- She was at our house.
I thought she was just part of the family.
She spoke Yiddish.
I mean, you know, there are no Ukrainians used to speak Yiddish.
But she did, because she was with us for a long time.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From then, I went to another farm-- my dad's good friend.
He happen-- he was not there.
Whether he escaped with the-- I don't know what happened to him, because he was gone.
And his nephew and, and his grandmother were on that farm.
I was there for about four or five days.
Where did they keep you?
I was in the house, and this was in a wooded area.
They-- and then while I was there-- I don't know how many days after I was gone.
I, I don't-- But I would say within a week, they told me that they are killing the Jews that day.
I remember when this-- I can't think of his name-- the nephew of that owner of the farm-- told me about that.
I know he was sad, too.
But I-- at that time, I was like-- I felt like I, I was-- I couldn't even cry anymore.
Just couldn't.
I mean, there just-- I was numb.
Well, not that it was a surprise.
I knew it was going to happen.
This is in Ostrozec?
Yes, in Ostrozec.
He says, they're killing the Jews in Ostrozec.
And at that time, it just like I wish I was there, too.
I mean, I, I had no will to live.
I just sat there and I just-- no matter what, they could do anything they want to me.
It didn't matter, because I thought of my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was there maybe a couple more days.
They, too, were afraid, and I had to go on.
No, I was-- I mean, there was-- this farm was very much an isolated-- I mean, there was a-- like a forest-- was a pine forest.
They owned ten hectares of forest, and I don't know how many acres that is.
It's a pretty good size.
And there were-- so nobody saw me going there, as far as I know.
But they were just afraid.
And I had to go on.
He said I couldn't stay too much longer here, he says.
So I took off, and I just wandered.
To day, I was mostly going from area to area.
Sometime I would just go into a house that I think maybe was fairly safe and get some food.
And, and at night, I would sneak in some barn, stay at somebody's barn.
They didn't know whether I was there or not.
The next day, I just walked again.
This was still in the fall.
Yes.
In wandering from place to place, I, I went into this farmer and, you know, wanted-- I hoped we-- they would give me some food, and they did.
And he says, you know, not far from here there's this area where there's maybe 200 or 300 Jews, he says, working there.
He said, maybe that's a good place for you to go.
Maybe they won't kill them anymore.
That's the way he said that.
Well, I thought there was just nothing.
All the Jews are killed.
So it was sort of surprising to me that he mentioned that.
And I kept going there.
And came up with, with like a, a top of the hill there's this huge-- it's almost like a castle.
And there was a Polish count at one time lived there, and I don't know how many acres.
It was run by a German-- occupied-- I mean, and there were Jews working on the farmland and whatever.
So when I got there-- I think it was on a Friday.
Because the reason I remember that day, because there's some Jewish women preparing for the Sabbath-- Friday afternoon.
And you know, like busybodies looking.
And I thought, God, these people are crazy.
I mean, you know, I know they're going to be killed.
But then again, but here I am.
At least I felt among Jews.
Felt-- So I don't know how, but somehow they-- some people asked me to stay there with them.
And-- Where was the farm?
Do you know the name of the town?
The farm-- it wasn't actually-- it was the vicinity.
It was not that many miles I, I-- it just-- I forgot the name of it.
I can't=- was just a unique place.
It was just-- really, I mean, if I look at it different, it was just a beautiful area on top of a hill.
There were a lot of buildings, and there was a row of houses where probably the workers used to live there years ago when they worked the farm.
So it was like barracks in a way.
But we were, you know, in one room, it was like about eight people.
And, and right after that, that weekend, the Sabbath and just a couple days after that, the woman where I stayed with worked for the German, for that lieutenant who was running the, the-- this farm.
I don't know whether she was the cook or cleaning, but she came in one night and she said that he told her that they're going to kill the Jews in that place.
So all night, we just-- we didn't-- nobody went to sleep.
We were just all sitting up, waiting.
I don't know, waiting to die, I guess, or-- what's going to happen.
Through the night, late at night, we saw activity like trucks.
We heard the sound of trucks and, and lights of trucks, which normally you don't see over there.
I think there were just no motorized vehicles there, except the lieutenant had a car and a chauffeur.
But this was trucks, a lot of them.
We heard that.
You know, nobody said much of anything, because it's like, you know, you, you, you just don't know what to say.
You know you're waiting to die.
Early in the morning, it was-- start getting daylight.
We saw the activity, and it was just getting so-- and I-- I walk out the door, and I say, like, I, I gotta run.
I started running down the hill.
And there was a road down the hill, and there were two policemen with rifles.
They were in uniform, and stopped me and pushed me back.
It's strange that I, I, I, I remember I smelled moonshine whiskey from these policemen.
They were like drinking all night.
And here I'm back.
And there was this open area on that huge farm.
I mean, it's a big place.
And I got-- there was turmoil, women screaming, children.
It was like they can't find their children, running, looking for their husbands.
And it just-- it's just turmoil.
And running back and forth, and they surrounding the place.
It's already surrounded, most of it.
I came to an area with a huge fence, and I thought, boy, if I could break out that fence.
It happened so that two adults did that.
And they knocked out a couple boards and they went through it.
And I did too.
And they were already firing shots after all of us.
And we were running through a field-- just an open field.
There was nothing but open spaces outside that.
And bullets were flying.
It's-- I mean, if I was going to die, I guess that would be as good a way to do it-- to die.
And we were running.
These guys were running, and I tried to follow them.
I was in back of them.
And this was a-- this was early in the morning.
We end up running and running and running all day long.
Till we-- we hit a wooded area.
And these two men, I didn't know them.
They didn't know me.
But they didn't want me to follow them.
And they tried to lose me.
And I kept on, you know, just following them And I-- and they finally lost me, so I was all by myself.
It was-- And this must have gone on-- this was all day.
Started in the morning, and this was already late in the afternoon.
Didn't need anything.
Didn't do anything.
Just scared to death.
But at least we got out of that.
And I don't know how many miles I was from that area.
I don't remember exactly.
But the next day, I stayed-- I slept outside in the wooded area.
The next day, I was not familiar with these villages.
I didn't know where I-- you know, so I--
